answers:
**1. Root cause of the cross-tenant leak.** The application connects to Postgres as the table-owning role, so row-level security is silently skipped. `cedarline/.env` (and `.env.example`) set `DATABASE_URL=postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar`, and `backend/app/db.py` builds the SQLAlchemy engine directly from that variable. `cedar_owner` is the `POSTGRES_USER` from `docker-compose.yml` — a superuser and the owner of every table created by `scripts/migrate.sh`. The RLS setup in `db/migrations/0001_init.sql` does `ALTER TABLE clients ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` with a `tenant_isolation` policy on `current_setting('app.current_tenant')`, but Postgres does not apply RLS policies to superusers or to table owners (absent `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`). So even though `backend/app/deps.py` dutifully runs `SET app.current_tenant = :tenant_id` per request, the policy is never evaluated on `cedar_owner`'s connections, and the unfiltered `SELECT ... FROM clients` in `backend/app/main.py` returns every tenant's rows. There is no error or warning — the isolation is simply inert.

**2. Correct fix and go-forward structure.** Point the running application at the least-privilege role the bootstrap already creates: change the app's connection string (the `DATABASE_URL` in `cedarline/.env`, and the default documented in `.env.example`) to `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar`. `cedar_app` (from `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`) has only SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE and no ownership or BYPASSRLS, so the `tenant_isolation` policies actually apply. Going forward, keep two distinct roles with two distinct connection strings: `cedar_owner` used **only** by `scripts/migrate.sh` for migrations, and `cedar_app` for everything the app process does. As defense in depth I'd also add `ALTER TABLE ... FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` in the migration so even owner connections are policy-checked, and tighten `deps.py` to use `SET LOCAL` inside a transaction (and close/return connections) so the tenant GUC can't leak across pooled connections.

**3. Why the app role disappears after a wipe.** `cedar_app` is created only by `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, which is explicitly a one-time manual step (`docs/local-setup.md` step 2; the file's own header says "Not part of automatic database initialization; run by hand on first setup"). The reset path, `scripts/reset-local-db.sh`, runs `docker compose down -v` — which deletes the `cedar_pgdata` volume and with it the entire Postgres cluster, including all roles — then brings the DB back up and runs only `scripts/migrate.sh`, which applies `db/migrations/*.sql` and never touches `db/bootstrap/`. So after a wipe, `cedar_app` no longer exists, and the isolation test suite (`backend/tests/conftest.py` defaults `TEST_DATABASE_URL` to `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar`) fails immediately with "role does not exist." To make it work again, the bootstrap must be re-run against the fresh cluster: `psql "postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar" -f db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`. Note the ordering matters: because the script also does `GRANT ... ON ALL TABLES` plus `ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES`, running the bootstrap before migrations (or re-running grants after) is needed so `cedar_app` has privileges on the recreated tables. The durable fix is to fold role creation into the reset flow — e.g. mount the bootstrap as a Postgres init script in `docker-compose.yml` or call it from `reset-local-db.sh` before `migrate.sh`.
report:
I have everything I need — the cedarline project's config, schema, app code, tests, and docs tell the whole story.

## Plan

Two reported issues in the multi-tenant clinic-scheduling backend (the `cedarline/` project): a cross-tenant leak on `GET /clients` despite row-level security, and the application role vanishing after a local DB rebuild. I set out to check (a) which database role the app actually connects as versus which role the RLS policies were designed for, and (b) how the app role gets created versus what the reset script actually recreates.

## Actions

1. Read `.claude/skills/environment-and-build-hazards/SKILL.md` — its "two-role model" section (owner/bypass roles silently skip RLS; app-only roles created by one-time init scripts don't survive a volume wipe) directly shaped what I looked for.
2. `find` over the working directory — identified `cedarline/` as the clinic-scheduling backend (other projects: `clinic-notes/`, `meadowdesk/` are unrelated).
3. Read `cedarline/.env`, `.env.example`, `docker-compose.yml`, `scripts/migrate.sh`, `scripts/reset-local-db.sh`, `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, `db/migrations/0001_init.sql` — found the app's `DATABASE_URL` uses `cedar_owner`, RLS policies on `clients`/`appointments`, and a manually-run bootstrap that creates `cedar_app`.
4. Read `backend/app/db.py`, `backend/app/deps.py`, `backend/app/main.py`, `backend/tests/conftest.py`, `backend/tests/test_isolation.py`, `docs/local-setup.md`, `README.md` — confirmed the app builds its engine from `DATABASE_URL` while the isolation tests connect as `cedar_app`.

## Answers

**1. Root cause of the cross-tenant leak.** The application connects to Postgres as the table-owning role, so row-level security is silently skipped. `cedarline/.env` (and `.env.example`) set `DATABASE_URL=postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar`, and `backend/app/db.py` builds the SQLAlchemy engine directly from that variable. `cedar_owner` is the `POSTGRES_USER` from `docker-compose.yml` — a superuser and the owner of every table created by `scripts/migrate.sh`. The RLS setup in `db/migrations/0001_init.sql` does `ALTER TABLE clients ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` with a `tenant_isolation` policy on `current_setting('app.current_tenant')`, but Postgres does not apply RLS policies to superusers or to table owners (absent `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY`). So even though `backend/app/deps.py` dutifully runs `SET app.current_tenant = :tenant_id` per request, the policy is never evaluated on `cedar_owner`'s connections, and the unfiltered `SELECT ... FROM clients` in `backend/app/main.py` returns every tenant's rows. There is no error or warning — the isolation is simply inert.

**2. Correct fix and go-forward structure.** Point the running application at the least-privilege role the bootstrap already creates: change the app's connection string (the `DATABASE_URL` in `cedarline/.env`, and the default documented in `.env.example`) to `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar`. `cedar_app` (from `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`) has only SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE and no ownership or BYPASSRLS, so the `tenant_isolation` policies actually apply. Going forward, keep two distinct roles with two distinct connection strings: `cedar_owner` used **only** by `scripts/migrate.sh` for migrations, and `cedar_app` for everything the app process does. As defense in depth I'd also add `ALTER TABLE ... FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` in the migration so even owner connections are policy-checked, and tighten `deps.py` to use `SET LOCAL` inside a transaction (and close/return connections) so the tenant GUC can't leak across pooled connections.

**3. Why the app role disappears after a wipe.** `cedar_app` is created only by `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`, which is explicitly a one-time manual step (`docs/local-setup.md` step 2; the file's own header says "Not part of automatic database initialization; run by hand on first setup"). The reset path, `scripts/reset-local-db.sh`, runs `docker compose down -v` — which deletes the `cedar_pgdata` volume and with it the entire Postgres cluster, including all roles — then brings the DB back up and runs only `scripts/migrate.sh`, which applies `db/migrations/*.sql` and never touches `db/bootstrap/`. So after a wipe, `cedar_app` no longer exists, and the isolation test suite (`backend/tests/conftest.py` defaults `TEST_DATABASE_URL` to `postgresql://cedar_app:apppw@localhost:5432/cedar`) fails immediately with "role does not exist." To make it work again, the bootstrap must be re-run against the fresh cluster: `psql "postgresql://cedar_owner:ownerpw@localhost:5432/cedar" -f db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql`. Note the ordering matters: because the script also does `GRANT ... ON ALL TABLES` plus `ALTER DEFAULT PRIVILEGES`, running the bootstrap before migrations (or re-running grants after) is needed so `cedar_app` has privileges on the recreated tables. The durable fix is to fold role creation into the reset flow — e.g. mount the bootstrap as a Postgres init script in `docker-compose.yml` or call it from `reset-local-db.sh` before `migrate.sh`.

## Next steps

If I could make changes I would: (1) switch the app `DATABASE_URL` in `.env`/`.env.example` to `cedar_app` and introduce a separate `MIGRATION_DATABASE_URL` for `migrate.sh`; (2) wire `db/bootstrap/create_app_role.sql` into `reset-local-db.sh` (before migrations) or as a docker-entrypoint init script, making the role creation idempotent (`DO $$ ... IF NOT EXISTS`); (3) add `FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY` to the tables; (4) fix `deps.py` to use `SET LOCAL` and properly release connections; (5) add an isolation test that actually queries as `cedar_app` and asserts only one tenant's rows come back.

## Confidence and gaps

High confidence on all three answers: the role mismatch, RLS owner/superuser bypass semantics, and the reset script's `down -v` without re-bootstrapping are all directly visible in the files cited. The main things I could not verify (read-only constraint): the live database state on the teammate's machine, whether `cedar_owner` is in fact superuser at runtime (it is, as the `POSTGRES_USER` of the official postgres image, but I didn't connect to confirm), and I did not run the tests to reproduce the exact "role does not exist" error. There's also no git history available in `cedarline/` (not a git repo) to confirm when the reset script was introduced.
